comments.text: I would like to share a short note and a Lean 4 formalization related to Erdős problem #691, both produced using GPT-5.4 Pro (which carried out the natural-language proof and its Lean formalization). The main statement proved is that for $A \subseteq \mathbb N$, the set $M_A$ has natural density $1$ if and only if $\lim_{N\to\infty} d(M_{A\cap[1,N]}) = 1$, equivalently if and only if $\sup_{F\subseteq A,\ F\text{ finite}} d(M_F)=1$. It seems quite plausible to me that the mathematics surrounding Problem #691 is already largely present in the existing literature on sets of multiples and Behrend sequences. At the same time, the relevant references appear to be spread across several sources, and I do not yet feel that I have completed a sufficiently comprehensive literature review to make a definitive priority claim. I would therefore especially welcome corrections concerning references, priority, and the exact relation of these notes to earlier work. * Main note: [PDF] * Lean file / repository: [GitHub] * typecheck information: [Lean 4 web]
Thanks! This is well known. See Hall and Tenenbaum, equation 1.3. Credit to GPT-5.4 Thinking for discussion.

Thanks for the reference. I have now checked Hall–Tenenbaum, and you are right that equation (1.3), cited there as a result of Davenport and Erdős, already contains the finite-approximation statement. I will revise the note to cite this reference explicitly and to frame the contribution as an expository write-up together with a Lean 4 formalization of this classical fact.
I think you might have misread the $n_k$ as $\eta_k$?
